name: Amniotic fluid volume at peak
tier_rationale: Tier A from Brace & Wolf 1989: AFV rises through mid-pregnancy to ~800 mL peak, falls toward term. Well-replicated reference trajectory.

name: Gestational week of peak amniotic fluid volume
tier_rationale: Tier A from Brace & Wolf 1989: AFV peak at 32-34 weeks; fetal-urine production and swallowing approach steady state.

source_check.source_citation: Beall & Ross, Amniotic Fluid Volume in Fetal Health and Disease (textbook chapter, via RadiologyKey), citing Brace & Wolf 1989
source_check.quote: They reported a peak AFV of 931 mL at 33.8 weeks with a decrease in the AFV thereafter
source_check.extracted_value: Secondary textbook chapter summarizing the Brace & Wolf (1989) nomogram reports the peak AFV occurring at 33.8 weeks, matching the stored peak week of 33.

name: Amniotic fluid volume at term
tier_rationale: Tier A from Brace & Wolf 1989: AFV declines from peak to ~600 mL at term; oligohydramnios threshold conventionally AFI<5 cm or single deepest pocket<2 cm.

source_check.source_citation: Beall & Ross, Amniotic Fluid Volume in Fetal Health and Disease (textbook chapter, via RadiologyKey), 50th-percentile AFV table (Sandlin et al. 2014)
source_check.quote: the same table shows 543.5 mL at 40 weeks' gestation at the 50th percentile
source_check.extracted_value: Secondary textbook chapter's 50th-percentile AFV table gives 543.5 mL at 40 weeks. This brackets the stored term central 600 mL. (Other reviews quote ~800 mL at 40 wk; nomograms differ, so the stored 600 mL sits within the cross-source spread.)

name: Amniotic fluid volume at 20 weeks
tier_rationale: Tier A from Brace & Wolf 1989: AFV at 20 weeks ~350 mL; interpolated reference point on the trajectory.

source_check.source_citation: Beall & Ross, Amniotic Fluid Volume in Fetal Health and Disease (textbook chapter, via RadiologyKey), 50th-percentile AFV table (Sandlin et al. 2014)
source_check.quote: Amniotic fluid volumes are in milliliters ... 425.8 mL at 20 weeks' gestation at the 50th percentile
source_check.extracted_value: Secondary textbook chapter's 50th-percentile AFV table gives 425.8 mL at 20 weeks. This brackets the stored central 350 mL (which sits below the median but within the normal spread for this gestational age).

name: Amniotic fluid volume early-pregnancy baseline
tier_rationale: Tier B from Brace & Wolf 1989: early-pregnancy (~12-14 weeks) AFV is ~50-200 mL; serves as the baseline anchor for the Gaussian-bump trajectory model. Wide CI reflects sparser early-pregnancy data.

source_check.source_citation: Ross & Beall, Amniotic Fluid: Physiology and Assessment, GLOW M (Global Library of Women's Medicine)
source_check.quote: At 12 weeks' gestation, the average volume is 60 ml. By 16 weeks, when genetic amniocentesis is often performed, the mean volume is 175 ml.
source_check.extracted_value: GLOWM review reports AF volume of 60 ml at 12 weeks and 175 ml at 16 weeks. These early-pregnancy figures bracket the stored early-baseline central of 100 mL (range 50-200).

notes: Used as the baseline offset in the amniotic_fluid_volume_trajectory submodel (Gaussian-bump approximation to the Brace & Wolf 1989 curve).

name: Amniotic fluid volume Gaussian-bump spread (sigma)
tier_rationale: Tier C: empirical Gaussian-fit spread parameter. Approximates the Brace & Wolf 1989 curve shape; the true curve is closer to a piecewise polynomial. The Gaussian approximation is documented in the submodel's reference kernel.
notes: Single-fit value, not from a meta-analysis. The downstream submodel inherits Tier C from this parameter.

name: Fetal swallowing rate at term
tier_rationale: Tier C from Pritchard 1966 (the classical paper on fetal swallowing and amniotic-fluid volume) and subsequent ultrasound estimates: term fetal swallowing volume is highly variable but centres ~500–1000 mL/24h, comparable in magnitude to fetal urine output. The wide range reflects both true variability and the difficulty of measurement — most modern estimates derive from indirect methods.

source_check.source_citation: Beall & Ross, Amniotic Fluid Volume in Fetal Health and Disease (textbook chapter, via RadiologyKey)
source_check.quote: An estimated 210 to 760 mL/day is swallowed by the near term fetus.
source_check.extracted_value: Secondary textbook chapter states the near-term fetus swallows an estimated 210 to 760 mL/day. The stored 700 mL/24h falls within this range.

notes: Swallowing matches urine output to a first approximation; the small residual (intra-membranous + transmembranous absorption) keeps AFV roughly constant week-to-week.

name: Amniotic fluid glucose at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Underwood, Gilbert & Sherman 2005 J Perinatol review of amniotic-fluid biochemistry (and the broader AF-composition literature it summarises): AF glucose falls from early-pregnancy values close to maternal serum to ~0.5 mmol/L (about 10% of maternal) at term, reflecting the increasing fraction of AF that originates as fetal urine. AF glucose <0.2 mmol/L raises concern for intra-amniotic infection.

source_check.source_citation: Oliveira et al. 2002, Biochemical profile of amniotic fluid (Braz J Med Biol Res 35:215-222), Table 1 (36-42 wk group)
source_check.extracted_value: Term (36-42 wk) AF glucose was 17.94 +/- 13.53 mg/dL (methods state glucose reported as mg/dl; table header 'mg/ml' is a typo). That mean equals ~1.0 mmol/L, and the +/-1SD span (~0.24-1.75 mmol/L) brackets the stored 0.5 mmol/L.

notes: Low AF glucose alongside elevated AF lactate is the classical biochemical signature of chorioamnionitis.

name: Amniotic fluid lactate at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Underwood, Gilbert & Sherman 2005 J Perinatol review of AF biochemistry: AF lactate at term centres ~3–5 mmol/L (roughly 3× maternal arterial), reflecting fetal/placental anaerobic metabolism and limited clearance from the amniotic compartment. Elevated values (>10 mmol/L) suggest intra-amniotic infection or fetal hypoxia.
notes: Reference range is wide; mode of delivery (labour) and time-to-assay both shift the measurement.

name: Amniotic fluid creatinine at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Underwood, Gilbert & Sherman 2005 J Perinatol AF biochemistry review (and earlier obstetric biochemistry literature it summarises): AF creatinine rises through pregnancy as fetal renal contribution dominates, reaching ~2 mg/dL at term — historically used as a fetal-maturity marker before lecithin/sphingomyelin replaced it.

source_check.source_citation: Oliveira et al. 2002, Biochemical profile of amniotic fluid (Braz J Med Biol Res 35:215-222), Table 1 (36-42 wk group)
source_check.quote: Creatinine (mg/dl) ... (36-42 weeks) 1.83 +/- 0.42 ... Creatinine values in the amniotic fluid that best represent fetal maturity are 1.5 to 2.0 mg/dl
source_check.extracted_value: Term (36-42 wk) AF creatinine was 1.83 +/- 0.42 mg/dL; the article also states the maturity range is 1.5 to 2.0 mg/dl. Stored 2.0 mg/dL sits at the top of both the +/-1SD span (1.41-2.25) and the cited maturity range.

notes: Term AF creatinine substantially exceeds maternal serum (~0.6 mg/dL), confirming the fetal-urine dominance of AF in late gestation.

name: Amniotic fluid osmolality at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Underwood, Gilbert & Sherman 2005 J Perinatol AF biochemistry review: AF osmolality at term centres ~260 mOsm/kg, ~25 mOsm/kg below maternal plasma, owing to the dominant contribution of dilute fetal urine. Early-pregnancy AF is approximately isotonic; the fall is gradual through T2/T3.

source_check.source_citation: Oliveira et al. 2002, Biochemical profile of amniotic fluid (Braz J Med Biol Res 35:215-222), Table 1
source_check.extracted_value: AF osmolality was 264.81 +/- 9.54 mOsm/l at 13-20 wk, 259.62 +/- 15.34 at 27-34 wk, and 237.54 +/- 31.42 at 36-42 wk. The stored 260 mOsm/kg falls within the term +/-1SD span (206-269) and matches the late-second/third-trimester means.

notes: Loss of the hypotonicity (osmolality rising toward maternal plasma) suggests rupture-of-membranes contamination or ascending infection.
